text: The surgeon performs a lateral terminal pancreato-jejunostomy using barbed suture material, completing the posterior pancreato-jejunal anastomosis in a total plan view.
Using laparoscopic techniques, the surgeon supports the Virson canals and stretches the barbed wires to complete the posterior wall of the anastomosis without assistance.

The surgeon performs an entherotomy in the jejunum using a monopolar bisturi, preparing to pass the drenthutter through the incision. The anterior plan is initiated using the same thread, aiming to solidify the Virson canals to the jejunum.
The surgeon completes the suture using a barbed wire, marked as resorbable three zeros, indicating the finalization of the suturing process.
The surgeon uses a wire to telescope the regional preference on the distal pancreas, with a visible lost strand serving as a tutor. The threads are left untied at the end of the procedure.

The surgeon tightens the sutures without assistance, completing the posterior suture line. An adequate enterotomy is performed, allowing placement of the lost drain in the jejunal segment. The surgeon begins the anterior suture line using self-locking barbed wires in a continuous fashion.
The surgeon performs a quick suture on the jejunum, followed by the extrusion of the specimen through a phallostil wound, revealing the excised tissue.

There are twenty-four ganglions in the lymphadenectomy, so four are positive. The patient will drink from the next day and eat in an adapted way. Meeting the third day of the amylase allows us to remove the drains and the patient will leave the hospital.
